text: Chapter 5 extends the numbered chord catalog past 26 to add chords 27-30 - four open-string-root voicings used for horn-backing fill-ins - and explains why the new vamp work abandons C major for G: the new shapes depend on guitar-friendly open-string roots only available in certain keys (New chords 27-30; Key choice for new vamps). This forces a critical and explicit caveat on the otherwise-universal transposition mandate - open-string vamp shapes are key-specific and must be selected per key rather than uniformly moved (Some shapes don't transpose). Bridges are then introduced as a distinct study category, taught through the same standard-on-top / new-on-bottom two-line format used throughout the book, with the explicit instruction to drill any hard progression until comfortable (Bridges with new chords; Drill through hard progressions). The chapter culminates - and Baker flags Lesson 16 as the capstone, 'the key to all of the other lessons' - in the dominant-as-leading-chord principle: every chord-to-chord connection in jazz can be built by inserting the appropriate dominant between them, organised into the three-category taxonomy of dominant-to-dominant, dominant-to-minor, and dominant-to-major connections (Dominant as leading chord; Three connection categories). The pedagogical end-goal is named explicitly: drilling these chord-connection patterns trains the ear so the student can hear any song and immediately know what reharmonisation to use (Learn to choose by ear).
